constraints: An array AAA is called sortable if it can be sorted in **strictly ascending order** with the use of the following operation any number of times:
Choose index iii and a **positive integer** XXX, and then update AiA_iAi​ to Aimod  XA_i \mod XAi​modX.
Strictly ascending order means that A1<A2<…<A∣A∣A_1 < A_2 < \ldots < A_{|A|}A1​<A2​<…<A∣A∣​.

Your task is to find the number of sortable subarrays of AAA, i.e. find how many pairs (L,R)(L, R)(L,R) exist such that 1≤L≤R≤N1 \le L \le R \le N1≤L≤R≤N and [AL,AL+1,…,AR][A_L, A_{L + 1}, \ldots, A_R][AL​,AL+1​,…,AR​] is sortable.
The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains NNN - the size of the array AAA.
The second line contains NNN integers - A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.
For each test case, output on a new line the number of sortable subarrays of AAA.
The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : All subarrays except the whole array are sortable. Some examples are below:

[1,3,3][1, 3, 3][1,3,3] : Choose i=1,X=1i = 1, X = 1i=1,X=1, array updated to [0,3,3][0, 3, 3][0,3,3]. Choose i=2,X=2i = 2, X = 2i=2,X=2, array updated to [0,1,3][0, 1, 3][0,1,3], which is sorted in strictly ascending order.
[2,1][2, 1][2,1] : Choose i=1,X=2i = 1, X = 2i=1,X=2, array updated to [0,1][0, 1][0,1], which is sorted in strictly ascending order.
